Integration Guide

Main Membership System Connection Specification

This document describes how the main membership system should connect to the top-up system using a signed, short-lived token flow, with automatic account provisioning into Supabase and local session creation after verification for both the customer top-up page and the shop-owner backoffice.

Audience: engineering team

What this document covers

  • recommended integration architecture
  • required token fields
  • security and replay protection
  • database changes in Supabase
  • customer direct entry with preassigned shop
  • owner direct backoffice entry

Overview

The main membership system should be treated as the source of truth for authentication, while the top-up system remains the source of truth for top-up workflow data. Instead of creating local user credentials manually, the top-up system should accept a signed, short-lived login token, verify it server-side, auto-provision or update the relevant local record in Supabase, and then issue its own local session cookie.

This approach avoids password sharing between systems, removes the need for a separate customer account creation flow, supports automatic onboarding for new members, allows the customer page to open with the correct shop already selected, and allows the shop owner to enter the backoffice directly without seeing a local login form first.

Implementation status

This design is now implemented in the top-up system for the customer portal using a signed token exchange endpoint. The current implementation uses HS256 with a shared secret for practical rollout speed. If needed later, this can be upgraded to asymmetric verification without changing the customer-facing flow.

Item Value
Exchange endpointPOST /api/auth/membership-login
Supported token typeJWT-like signed token using HS256
Portal bootstrapCustomer and owner pages can accept ?membershipToken=... or #membershipToken=...
Local login resultTop-up system creates its own local session cookie after verification

Why this model is recommended

Safer than encrypted URL passwords

Sensitive credentials should not be carried inside URLs. A signed token is easier to validate, rotate, audit, expire, and protect against replay.

Works with Supabase

The local database can keep a linked customer profile, audit login attempts, and continue using the existing transaction and approval workflow with minimal disruption.

Supports automatic provisioning

If the member does not exist in the local database yet, the top-up system creates the local record on first successful login and updates it later on repeat logins.

Recommended architecture

1

Main system login

User signs in on the main membership system.

2

Create signed token

Main system generates a short-lived token for the top-up portal.

3

Enter top-up portal

Browser opens the top-up portal with the token bootstrap input.

4

Verify and provision

Top-up backend verifies claims, prevents replay, and upserts the customer record in Supabase.

5

Create local session

Top-up backend creates its own signed session cookie and sends the user directly into the correct portal.

Trust and security model

Preferred approach

  • main system signs token with a private key
  • top-up system verifies with a public key
  • token lifetime should be short, preferably minutes
  • token must include a unique jti for replay protection
  • top-up system should store only token metadata and a hash, never the raw token

Key protections

  • signature validation to prove sender authenticity
  • issuer and audience validation to prevent token misuse
  • expiry check to limit time window
  • single-use enforcement using jti
  • local session issuance only after full verification

Do not send passwords inside the URL. The token should prove trust and identity by signature, not by exposing a shared password.

Environment variables required by the top-up system

Variable Purpose
MEMBERSHIP_TOKEN_ENABLEDEnable or disable membership token login.
MEMBERSHIP_TOKEN_SECRETShared HMAC secret used to verify the incoming token signature.
MEMBERSHIP_TOKEN_ISSUERExpected token issuer string.
MEMBERSHIP_TOKEN_AUDIENCEExpected token audience string.
MEMBERSHIP_TOKEN_CLOCK_SKEW_SECONDSAllowed small timestamp skew during verification.
MEMBERSHIP_SYNC_ENDPOINTMembership-system API endpoint that receives approved top-up credit events.
MEMBERSHIP_SYNC_SECRETShared HMAC secret used to sign approval callback requests.
MEMBERSHIP_SYNC_TIMEOUT_MSTimeout for the approval callback request.

Token payload contract

The token should contain only the fields required for login, identity linkage, and initial provisioning. Stable IDs are more important than display names.

Field Required Description
issYesIssuer. Must match the main membership system.
audYesAudience. Must match the top-up system.
subYesStable external member identifier from the main system.
jtiYesUnique token ID for replay protection.
iatYesIssued-at timestamp.
nbfYesNot-before timestamp.
expYesExpiry timestamp.
portal_roleYesMust be either customer or owner.
member_codeCustomer onlyLocal customer/member code used by the top-up portal.
membership_statusCustomer onlyExpected values such as active, inactive, suspended.
shop_idYesExternal shop identifier from the membership system.
shop_codeRecommendedShop code used for local matching or provisioning.
shop_nameYesDisplay name of the selected shop.
owner_login or owner_codeOwner onlyIdentifier to show in the owner session and use for shop-owner mapping.
phoneRecommendedUsed for local customer profile sync and support operations.
full_nameRecommendedUsed for optional local display/profile information.
source_systemRecommendedAudit clarity when more than one upstream system exists.

Customer token spec

Use the customer token when a logged-in member clicks into a specific shop from the membership webapp and should be taken directly to the top-up page for that shop, without seeing a login form or a shop selector.

Field Required Notes
issYesMust equal the configured issuer, for example main-membership-system.
audYesMust equal the configured audience, for example topup-portal.
subYesStable external member ID.
jtiYesUnique token ID. Must not be reused.
iat, nbf, expYesShort-lived token timing fields.
portal_roleYesMust be customer.
member_codeYes8-digit customer/member code used by the top-up system.
membership_statusYesCurrent implementation accepts only active.
shop_idYesExternal shop identifier from the membership system.
shop_nameYesThe selected shop name to display and map locally.
shop_codeRecommendedHelps local matching if a shop already exists in the top-up system.
phoneRecommendedCustomer phone number for local profile sync.
full_nameRecommendedCustomer display name.
source_systemRecommendedFor audit and support tracing.
 
{
  "iss": "main-membership-system",
  "aud": "topup-portal",
  "sub": "member-7f34d812",
  "jti": "f2dc0f97-baf7-4df8-9cd9-4e7a4b5cb812",
  "iat": 1736400000,
  "nbf": 1736400000,
  "exp": 1736400300,
  "portal_role": "customer",
  "member_code": "63936541",
  "membership_status": "active",
  "shop_id": "shop-001",
  "shop_code": "POC001",
  "shop_name": "表嫂美食",
  "phone": "8536XXXXXXX",
  "full_name": "CHAN TAI MAN",
  "source_system": "main-membership-web"
}

Entry URL for customer: 

https://your-topup-domain.example/#membershipToken=<signed-customer-token>

Result:

  • customer login page is skipped
  • top-up page opens directly
  • shop is preassigned from the token
  • customer cannot switch shop in this integrated path
  • submitted record goes to the assigned shop automatically

Owner token spec

Use the owner token when a logged-in shop owner clicks the backoffice link from the membership website and should be taken directly into the correct shop-owner backoffice without seeing the local owner login page.

Field Required Notes
issYesMust equal the configured issuer.
audYesMust equal the configured audience.
subYesStable external owner/member ID.
jtiYesUnique token ID. Must not be reused.
iat, nbf, expYesShort-lived token timing fields.
portal_roleYesMust be owner.
shop_idYesExternal shop identifier from the membership system.
shop_nameYesShop display name.
shop_codeRecommendedShop code used for local matching/provisioning.
owner_login or owner_codeRecommendedOwner identifier to display in the local owner session.
full_nameRecommendedOwner display name for future UI use.
phoneRecommendedOwner phone if available.
source_systemRecommendedFor audit and support tracing.
 
{
  "iss": "main-membership-system",
  "aud": "topup-portal",
  "sub": "owner-2fa93bd1",
  "jti": "2d511f01-0ac4-4bd2-bc23-c6f3025d9151",
  "iat": 1736400000,
  "nbf": 1736400000,
  "exp": 1736400300,
  "portal_role": "owner",
  "shop_id": "shop-001",
  "shop_code": "POC001",
  "shop_name": "表嫂美食",
  "owner_login": "60000000",
  "full_name": "SHOP OWNER",
  "phone": "8536XXXXXXX",
  "source_system": "main-membership-web"
}

Entry URL for owner: 

https://your-topup-domain.example/owner.html#membershipToken=<signed-owner-token>

Result:

  • owner login page is skipped
  • owner enters the correct backoffice directly
  • the shop context is already attached

Recommended portal entry method

Preferred

Use a short-lived token and send it to a backend exchange endpoint, or use a fragment-based redirect such as #token=... and immediately exchange it on page load.

Why not query password URLs

Query parameters are more likely to appear in browser history, server logs, analytics, and referrer chains. Tokens should be short-lived and removed from visible URLs immediately after exchange.

Backend verification flow

  1. Receive token through the new membership login endpoint.
  2. Read token header and select the correct verification key.
  3. Verify signature using an allowlisted algorithm only.
  4. Validate iss, aud, sub, jti, iat, nbf, and exp.
  5. Reject the token if portal_role is not customer.
  6. Reject the token if membership status is not active.
  7. Check whether the same jti has already been used.
  8. Upsert the local customer record in Supabase.
  9. Create the existing local session cookie for the top-up system.
  10. Return the normal logged-in user payload to the frontend.

Main-system request example

The main system may either redirect the browser with a token in the fragment, or directly POST the token to the exchange endpoint from the frontend. The simplest browser redirect form is: 

Customer: https://your-topup-domain.example/#membershipToken=<signed-customer-token>
Owner:    https://your-topup-domain.example/owner.html#membershipToken=<signed-owner-token>

The top-up page will detect the token, exchange it with the backend, create a local session, then clean the token out of the browser URL.

Direct backend exchange payload:

POST /api/auth/membership-login
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "token": "<signed-token>"
}

Successful response shape: 

{
  "user": {
    "role": "customer or owner",
    "memberCode": "63936541 or null",
    "ownerLogin": "60000000 or null",
    "authSource": "membership",
    "externalMemberId": "external subject",
    "fullName": "display name",
    "phone": "phone number",
    "shopId": "local shop uuid",
    "shopName": "表嫂美食",
    "shopCode": "POC001"
  }
}

Approval callback API

After a shop owner approves a top-up, the top-up system now calls the membership system before finalizing the approval. If the membership callback fails, the transaction remains unapproved.

Item Value
DirectionTop-up system → Membership system
MethodPOST
URLMEMBERSHIP_SYNC_ENDPOINT
TriggerOnly when a transaction is approved
Behavior on failureApproval is blocked and the transaction stays pending

Headers sent by the top-up system:

Header Description
Content-Typeapplication/json
X-Topup-EventFixed value: topup_approved
X-Topup-TimestampISO timestamp used in request signing
X-Topup-SignatureHMAC SHA256 hex digest of {timestamp}.{rawBody} using MEMBERSHIP_SYNC_SECRET

Callback payload:

{
  "event": "topup_approved",
  "transaction_id": "9c5f6947-4d8d-4c47-a725-2c4d44a7f0f3",
  "member_code": "63936541",
  "total_amount": 300,
  "currency": "MOP",
  "item_count": 2,
  "approved_at": "2026-07-01T10:15:30.000Z",
  "owner_login": "60000000",
  "shop": {
    "id": "local-shop-uuid",
    "code": "POC001",
    "name": "表嫂美食",
    "external_shop_id": "shop-001"
  },
  "items": [
    {
      "id": "line-item-id",
      "merchant_name": "表嫂美食",
      "transaction_order_no": "2026062411365300000010",
      "amount": 300,
      "transaction_time": "2026-06-24 11:40:00",
      "payment_method": "中國銀行澳門分行(6756)"
    }
  ]
}

Expected membership-system success response:

{
  "ok": true,
  "credited": true,
  "reference_id": "membership-credit-000123"
}

Any HTTP 2xx response is treated as success. Non-2xx responses block approval and are stored in the top-up system transaction sync fields for troubleshooting.

Main-system signing example

The current implementation expects an HS256-signed token. Example pseudocode in Node.js: 

import crypto from "node:crypto";

function base64Url(input) {
  return Buffer.from(JSON.stringify(input)).toString("base64url");
}

function signMembershipToken(secret, payload) {
  const header = { alg: "HS256", typ: "JWT" };
  const signingInput = `${base64Url(header)}.${base64Url(payload)}`;
  const signature = crypto
    .createHmac("sha256", secret)
    .update(signingInput)
    .digest("base64url");

  return `${signingInput}.${signature}`;
}

Callback signature verification pseudocode for the membership system: 

import crypto from "node:crypto";

function verifyTopupCallbackSignature(secret, timestamp, rawBody, receivedSignature) {
  const expected = crypto
    .createHmac("sha256", secret)
    .update(`${timestamp}.${rawBody}`)
    .digest("hex");

  return expected === receivedSignature;
}

Supabase data model changes

Customer account linkage

Extend customer_accounts to support externally provisioned users rather than only local password login. Suggested new fields:

  • auth_source
  • external_member_id
  • full_name
  • phone
  • membership_status
  • profile_json
  • provisioned_at
  • last_login_at
  • last_synced_at

Replay and audit table

Create a table such as membership_token_logins to track token usage. Suggested fields:

  • jti unique
  • token_hash
  • issuer
  • audience
  • subject
  • member_code
  • status
  • reject_reason
  • expires_at
  • used_at
  • claims_json

Provisioning rules

Scenario Required behavior
New customer Automatically create local record in Supabase and log the customer in.
Existing customer with same external identity Update profile metadata if needed and log the customer in.
Existing customer with conflicting external identity Reject login, log the conflict, and require manual review.
Inactive or suspended member Reject login and do not create a local session.

Current implementation target inside the top-up system

The current project already has local session handling, customer and owner portals, Supabase-backed transactions, and protected APIs. The integration is added as a login bootstrap layer rather than rewriting the full application.

Frontend

Customer portal checks for a membership token on page load, exchanges it, receives a local session, fills the assigned shop automatically, and skips the local login form. Owner portal does the same and opens directly into the backoffice.

Backend

The new route /api/auth/membership-login verifies token, provisions customer and shop data, and writes the current local session cookie for either the customer or owner portal.

Database

Add identity-linkage fields and replay/audit logging in Supabase, plus shop linkage fields so the selected shop in the main system maps directly into the correct top-up record destination.

Integration steps for the main system

  1. Agree the issuer, audience, algorithm, and key rotation approach with the top-up system.
  2. Generate a signed short-lived token only after the user has passed the main system login process.
  3. Include the correct role and the target shop claims in the token payload.
  4. For customer entry, send the user to the top-up portal entry URL for the selected shop.
  5. For owner entry, send the user directly to the owner backoffice URL.
  6. Do not send raw passwords or long-lived secrets in the URL.
  7. Keep token expiry short and ensure jti is unique per issued token.
  8. If the top-up system rejects the token, surface the error to the user or redirect to a controlled failure path.

Operational checklist

Before go-live

  • define token claim contract
  • define issuer and audience values
  • configure public key or shared secret securely
  • add Supabase schema changes
  • build replay protection
  • test conflict and inactive-user scenarios

After go-live

  • monitor rejected token reasons
  • monitor replay attempts by jti
  • review account linkage conflicts
  • rotate keys safely using kid
  • periodically clean audit records according to retention policy

Scope boundary for this phase

The implemented phase now covers both:

  • customer direct entry with shop preassigned from the membership system
  • owner direct entry into the backoffice with shop context already attached

main membership system → signed token → direct portal entry without local login page
